title: Update to go 1.21
body:
So that we can continue updating our dependencies.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *Luap99* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.

Uh yes, they only ship go 1.20 there?

>  golang                   x86_64 1.20.12-4.el9_3        appstream         616 k
@lsm5 Can we ignore the epel-9 error? How is golang sourced there, I see much newer versions in centos 9 stream?

> @lsm5 Can we ignore the epel-9 error? How is golang sourced there, I see much newer versions in centos 9 stream?

the `epel` targets on copr are actually `rhel + epel`, so they are behind actual centos stream. We could ignore the epel failures.

An alternative would be that I could update our copr with a golang build from centos-stream and that could be used for these `epel` build jobs.

> An alternative would be that I could update our copr with a golang build from centos-stream and that could be used for these `epel` build jobs.

@Luap99 I'm building it at https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/rhcontainerbot/podman-next/build/7340248/ . Once that's ready, I think we can retrigger the epel build jobs here.

> the epel targets on copr are actually rhel + epel, so they are behind actual centos stream. We could ignore the epel failures.

Yeah but shouldn't even RHEL have newer golang versions available now? At least that is what I noticed by skimming our RHEL Jira issues from time to time.

> An alternative would be that I could update our copr with a golang build from centos-stream and that could be used for these epel build jobs.

That seems pointless given the goal it to actually build what the distro contains, if you use a different version what are we testing? We know that we can build it in our CI tests.

> > the epel targets on copr are actually rhel + epel, so they are behind actual centos stream. We could ignore the epel failures.
> 
> Yeah but shouldn't even RHEL have newer golang versions available now? At least that is what I noticed by skimming our RHEL Jira issues from time to time.

I'll have to check on that with the copr and rhel folks.

> 
> > An alternative would be that I could update our copr with a golang build from centos-stream and that could be used for these epel build jobs.
> 
> That seems pointless given the goal it to actually build what the distro contains, if you use a different version what are we testing? We know that we can build it in our CI tests.

Right, but then there will be this constant issue of some test or the other failing. As long as we're aware we're only ignoring known issues and others haven't crept up in the process. We can set the target to `centos-stream` and that might get us a successful build but that target won't be useful for RHEL testing.

Another option would be to have both `centos-stream` and `epel` build jobs. If stream passes and epel fails, we can be reasonably sure the epel builds will pass after a few days and can be mostly ignored. When it comes to TMT tests, we could do the same, have centos-stream tests use centos-stream targets [I hope this is doable], and rhel tests use the epel targets.
